title: National Highways Authority Of India vs M/S Sahakar Global Ltld., on 19 September, 2019
body: National Highways Authority Of India vs M/S Sahakar Global Ltld., on 19 September, 2019 Author: J. Uma Devi Bench: J. Uma Devi THE HON'BLE SRI JUSTICE M.SEETHARAMA MURTI AND THE HON'BLE MS. JUSTICE J.UMA DEVI Civil Revision Petition no.849 of 2019 ORDER: (Per Hon'ble Sri Justice M.Seetharama Murti) The unsuccessful petitioner filed this Civil Revision Petition, under Article 227 of the Constitution of India, assailing the order, dated 06.02.2019, of the learned Principal District Judge, West Godavari District at Eluru, passed in I.A.no.2303 of 2018 in Arbitration OPSR no.15853 of 2017. 2. We have heard the submissions of Sri Vedula Venkata Ramana, learned senior counsel appearing for Sri A.Laxminarayana, learned counsel appearing for the petitioner; and, of Sri B.Chandrasen Reddy, learned counsel appearing for the 1st respondent. The 2nd respondent, who is the sole Arbitrator, is a proforma party. We have perused the material record. 3. To begin with, it is necessary to state the relevant introductory facts: 'The Arbitral Tribunal/Sole Arbitrator passed an award, dated 22.05.2018. Aggrieved of the same, petitioner herein, who is the unsuccessful respondent, preferred the Arbn. OPSR aforementioned along with an application under Section 34(3) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 ('the Act', for brevity) for condonation of delay of (18) days in filing the said OPSR. The said application for condonation of delay was resisted by the respondents. By the order impugned, the learned District Judge dismissed the application for condonation of delay. Aggrieved thereof, the present revision is filed. 3.1 In this revision, the parties shall hereinafter be referred to as the petitioner and respondents as arrayed in this revision. MSRM, J& JUD J C.R.P.no.849 of 2019 2 4. In support of the request for condonation of delay, the case of the petitioner is this: 'The deponent is the Project Director of the petitioner/National Highways Authority of India. The OPSR is filed to set aside the arbitration award on various grounds. The award was sent to the office of the petitioner, on 23.05.2018, through mail by the counsel at Delhi, who appeared on behalf of the petitioner before the Tribunal. The award was scrutinized at various levels, that is, at the levels of the Project Implementation Unit [PIU], Regional office, Vijayawada, and Head Office at Delhi. Eventually, it is decided to challenge the Award. The scrutiny of the award at various levels, the correspondence and the decision making has taken considerable time. Meanwhile, the Project Director was transferred and the present incumbent, who is the deponent of the affidavit filed in support of the petition, took charge on 05.07.2018. As the arbitration proceedings and passing of the award were prior to his taking charge, he was not aware of the same. While reviewing the cases and examining various issues pertaining to PIU, it was noticed that the OP challenging the award has not been filed; and, immediately the matter was entrusted to one of the Standing Counsel and further steps were taken for filing petition under Section 34 of the Act. The said application ought to have been filed within 90 days from the date of receipt of the award, that is, on or before 20.08.2018. However, in the facts & circumstances stated, the delay of (18) days had occasioned. Hence, the present petition is filed for condonation of the said delay. The delay is not due to wilful and deliberate reasons. The delay had occasioned for the reasons stated above. The petitioner has a very good case in the matter and is having fair chances of success. Hence, the delay may be condoned and the OPSR may be registered and entertained.' 5. The case of the 1st respondent, in brief, is as follows: - 'The sole arbitrator was wrongly impleaded as 2nd respondent. The award is a well- MSRM, J& JUD J C.R.P.no.849 of 2019 3 reasoned and considered award and is sustainable under facts and in law. The award was passed on 22.05.2018. It was duly received by the petitioner, on 23.05.2018. The petitioner failed to file the OPSR within a period of three months in terms of Section 34(3) of the Act. It is pertinent to take note of the lackadaisical conduct of the petitioner in filing the OPSR before the District Court, on 07.09.2018, without filing an application for condonation of delay. This respondent learnt that many objections were raised by the office of the District Court at the time of scrutiny of the OPSR. The petitioner filed the subject application for condonation of delay on 01.10.2018 after the defect was pointed out by the office of the District Court. The petitioner did not file the application for condonation of delay along with the OPSR before the District Court. The intention of the petitioner is to somehow keep the application under Section 34 of the Act pending to avoid enforcement of the Award. The present OPSR is hopelessly barred by limitation, as it was filed beyond the period of limitation. The explanation offered by the deponent of the affidavit for condonation of delay is false. The deponent made bald and bare allegations without presenting any cogent material for showing sufficient cause for condonation of delay in filing the OPSR. Each day's delay is not satisfactorily explained. The reasons for delay furnished are flimsy and invented. The OPSR is filed with delay to harass this respondent. The delay of 18 days cannot be condoned and the application for condonation of delay is liable for dismissal and the OPSR is liable for rejection. The contention that the delay is neither wilful nor wanton nor deliberate is false. The petitioner has good case and fair chance of success in the OPSR is not correct. This respondent lodged a caveat before the Delhi High Court anticipating likely initiation of proceedings under Section 34 of the Act by the petitioner. The petitioner deliberately filed the present petition before the District Court, Eluru, which is not having territorial jurisdiction. MSRM, J& JUD J C.R.P.no.849 of 2019 4 6. At the hearing, learned senior counsel appearing for both the sides reiterated the respective contentions of the parties. 7. The admitted and undisputed factual matrix is this: The learned Sole Arbitrator/2nd respondent passed the award, dated 22.05.2018. Aggrieved of the said award, the petitioner filed OPSR before the District Court, West Godavari District, at Eluru. Since delay had occasioned, the petitioner filed the subject application under Section 34(3) of the Act for condonation of delay of 18 days. The reasons assigned for condonation of delay are already stated supra while extracting the pleadings of the petitioner. Similarly, the case of the contesting respondent is also stated supra. 8. It is profitable to first refer to Section 34(3) of the Act, which is relevant and which reads as under: '34 (3) An application for setting aside may not be made after three months have elapsed from the date on which the party making that application had received the arbitral award or, if a request had been made under Section 33 , from the date on which that request had been disposed of by the arbitral tribunal: Provided that if the Court is satisfied that the applicant was prevented by sufficient cause from making the application within the said period of three months it may entertain the application within a further period of thirty days but not thereafter.' 9. From the submissions, it is discernible that the OPSR was filed unaccompanied by a petition for condonation of delay. At the time of scrutiny of the OPSR, the office of the District Court pointed out certain defects including the defect that the OPSR is not filed within the time allowed under law. Therefore, the petitioner filed the subject application for condonation of delay of 18 days. 10. In this regard, the first submission of the learned counsel appearing for the 1st respondent is that since the OPSR was originally filed with delay, however, unaccompanied by an application for condonation of delay in filing MSRM, J& JUD J C.R.P.no.849 of 2019 5 the said OPSR, the OPSR is liable for rejection and that the subsequent filing of application for condonataion of delay will not cure the defect and will not inure to the benefit of the petitioner. It is also pertinent to note that when the office of the District Court, after scrutiny, returned the OPSR with certain office objections, the petitioner subsequently submitted an application for condonation of delay of 18 days and re-presented the OPSR. There was also delay in re-filing the OPSR with the petition for condonation of delay. Therefore, for condonation of the said delay in re-filing the OPSR (accompanied by an application for condonation of delay), an application is filed for condonation of delay in re-filing. That application was allowed by the District Court and the delay in re-filing is condoned is not in dispute. However, the application for condonation of delay in filing the OPSR was later dismissed by the impugned order. Be that as it may. 11. Since there was also delay in re-filing, though the said delay was condoned by the Court below, learned senior counsel appearing for the 1st respondent would contend that though the delay in re-filing is condoned, yet, as the OPSR was re-presented/re-filed beyond the period of three months provided under law, the delay is not condonable. 12. Now the first short question is as to whether the application for condonation of delay in filing the OPSR under Section 34 of the Act would inure for the benefit of the petitioner though the said application for condonation of delay was not filed at the time of original filing of the OPSR. It is also to be incidentally examined as to whether the delay in re-filing, which was though condoned, would disentitle the petitioner to seek condonation of delay of 18 days in filing the OPSR. 13. In this background of facts and for answering the questions, it is necessary to refer to the decisions cited since facts and submissions are already MSRM, J& JUD J C.R.P.no.849 of 2019 6 narrated supra. Reliance is placed on the following decisions by the learned senior counsel for the 1st respondent: In Green Valley C.G.H.S Ltd. V. Gurucharan Singh1, the facts and the ratio are as follows: 'Aggrieved of an award, the petitioner in the cited case filed a petition under Section 34 of the Act. The office of the Court returned that application with objections. The petition was re-filed within the time, on 27.10.2014, as the Court reopened after Diwali holidays on that day. Again, the OP was returned, on 30.10.2014, for removal of objections. The petition remained lying under objections. The Registry of the Court raised objections from time to time. But, all the objections were not removed. The petition was refiled, on 16.01.2015, without removing the objections raised by the Registry. Again, the petition was taken from the Registry and was re-filed, on 28.01.2015, but the objections raised by the Registry were yet not removed. Finally, after removing the defects, the petition was re-filed on 04.04.2015, and came to be listed before the Court on 08.04.2015, with a petition for condonation of delay in re-filing. The actual number of days of delay in re- filing was noted as 159 days, that is, after expiry of three months' period prescribed under Section 34 of the Act. However, it was also noted that the petitioner in the application for condonaton of delay mentioned the delay as 118 days. It was also further noted that for only certain period of delay, the reasons are mentioned; but for the subsequent period of delay, that is, from 22.12.2014 to 04.04.2015, no grounds were mentioned in the application. The Court in the cited decision observed that in any case, after expiry of 30 days after the statutory of three months, the delay of even one day cannot be condoned if the objections are not filed within three months or along with an application for condonation of delay of 30 days as provided under Section 34(3) of the Act. And that in arbitration matters, the limitation is to be strictly construed and there is not an iota of doubt that otherwise the very purpose 1 2016 (157) DRJ 105 MSRM, J& JUD J C.R.P.no.849 of 2019 7 would be frustrated if the delay after extended period is condoned and that the exercise of power under Section 151 of the Code is to prevent the abuse of the process of the Court. Finally, it was observed following the decision in Delhi Development Authority v. M/s. Durga Construction Company [2013(139) DRJ 133] that it appears that if sufficient cause is shown, the delay in re-filing after the expiry of 30 days period is condonable subject to the condition, if a strong case is made out and that in that particular case, no sufficient cause has been shown by the petitioner therein. Thus, this decision was eventually rendered having regard to the facts of the case. In the decision in Mahanagar Telephone Nigam Ltd., Delhi v. Prakash & Co. Construction Company2 , the facts and the ratio are as follows: Aggrieved of an arbitral award, a petition was filed under Section 34 of the Act. When the said petition was returned, the same was re-filed along with a petition under Section 151 of the Code for condonation of delay of 188 days in re-filing the petition under section 34 of the Act. The respondent therein contended that delay is not 188 days but 233 days and hence, the delay in re-filing cannot be condoned. Having noted that in the cited case, it took the petitioner about seven months twenty days to remove all the objections raised by the Registry and as three months' period had already expired, on 15.05.2012, as per the case of the petitioner, the Court took the view that for the purpose of limitation period, the provisions of Section 34 of the Act are mandatory and the delay of this nature cannot be condoned. This decision is also rendered having regard to the facts peculiar to the case. In Delhi Transco Ltd. V.Hythro Engineers Pvt. Ltd.3, the facts and ratio are as follows: Against an award, petition under Section 34 of the Act was filed. When the petition was returned, an application under Section 151 of the Code was filed for condonation of delay in re-filing. A learned Single Judge 2 197 (2013) DLT 772 3 2012 (3) ARAB LR 349 (Delhi) MSRM, J& JUD J C.R.P.no.849 of 2019 8 dismissed the said petition. Therefore, the matter went to a Division Bench of the High Court of Delhi. The Division Bench noted that there was 72 days delay in re-filing and that the ground taken in the application to explain the delay was as under: 'That the objection petition under defect was collected from the Registry by the junior counsel of the appellant's counsel as the Clerk of the appellant's counsel was on leave during the relevant period and that the objection petition was mistakenly tagged with some other files and, therefore, remained untraceable till 18.09.2011 and that the office of the counsel started reconstructing the record for fresh filing. That, on 19.09.2011, when the counsel was searching for some other files, he suddenly found the objection petition. Thereafter, the same was re-filed.' It was also submitted that the delay in re-filing the objection petition was not deliberate or intentional, and had occurred due to bona fide reasons as aforesaid. The Division Bench noted that the learned Single Judge examined the facts of the case in the light of the decisions and concluded that the appellant had failed to disclose sufficient reasons/cause for justifying the condonation of delay in re-filing the petition. The Division Bench took note of the fact that the learned Single Judge rightly observed that neither the affidavit of the Junior Advocate, who allegedly misplaced the objection petition after taking it back under defect), nor the Clerk had been filed in support of the application. Condonation of delay in re- filing the objection petition in these facts should run against the intention of the Parliament and the statutory scheme under the Act. The Division Bench further held as follows: 'Moreover, there is no answer with the appellant to the reliance placed by the learned Single Judge on Rule 5, Chapter "1", Part A of Volume 5 of High Court Rules and Orders, according to which the objections should have been re-filed within a time not exceeding 7 days at a time, and 30 days in aggregate to be fixed by the Deputy Registrar/Assistant Registrar, In charge of Filing Counter.' Thus, this decision was rendered having regard to the facts and the rules of the said High Court. MSRM, J& JUD J C.R.P.no.849 of 2019 9 14. The above three decisions, which were rendered having regard to the facts peculiar to the cases, are also not helpful to the present respondent in the considered view of this Court. Moreover, the decisions of the Delhi High Court are only of persuasive value. Furthermore, in the case on hand, admittedly, the delay in re-filing the OPSR is already condoned by the learned District Judge and the said order has become final and remained unchallenged and as between the parties, the said order is binding and estops the respondent from challenging the said order. An order, which is erroneous, even if remained unchallenged, becomes final and binding on the parties is undisputable. Hence, the contention of the learned counsel for the 1st respondent that the OPSR accompanied by application for condonation of delay of 18 days is liable for rejection on the ground that the OPSR filed by the petitioner was once returned with the objections of the office of the Court below and that there was delay in re-filing is not tenable and needs no countenance, having regard to the fact that the delay in re-filing is already condoned whereas in the decisions relied upon on behalf of the 1st respondent the facts reflect that the applications for condonation of delay in re-filing were dismissed, having regard to the facts of the individual cases. 15. Per contra, learned Senior Counsel appearing for the petitioner relied upon a Division Bench decision of the High Court of Judicature at Hyderabad, rendered on 13.07.2018, in C.R.P.no.3483 of 2018. The facts and the ratio in the cited decision are as follows: 'It is a revision preferred under Article 227 of the Constitution of India, against the order, dated 27.02.2018, of the learned Chief Judge, City Civil Court, Hyderabad, in I.A.no.4785 of 2017 in OPSR 25212 of 2016. An application to set aside the award was filed, on 08.11.2016, beyond three months' period stipulated in Section 34(3) of the Act. The said three months' period expired on 10.10.2016. The said application, however, was filed within the extended period of 30 days as stipulated in the proviso to MSRM, J& JUD J C.R.P.no.849 of 2019 10 Section 34(3) of the Act. The fact, however, remains is that along with the application filed under Section 34 of the Act, the petitioner did not file application for condonation of delay in filing the Original Petition beyond 90 days and within 120 days. The petition was returned on 15.11.2016, but was re-presented only 10 months thereafter, on 19.09.2017. The Division Bench noted that the petition to set aside the award, under Section 34(3) of the Act, was filed on 08.11.2016, that is, 28 days, beyond the three months' period stipulated under Section 34(3) of the Act, but within the extended period of 30 days and that on its being returned, an application to condone delay was filed thereafter along with an application to condone delay in re-presenting. The Division Bench also noted that the petition under Section 34 of the Act was filed within the extended period of 30 days, that is, after the expiry of three months period stipulated under Section 34 of the Act and that therefore, filing an application to condone delay thereafter would not mean that the petition was filed beyond limitation. The Division Bench took the view that mere fact that the application to condone delay was filed thereafter was not fatal and accordingly set aside the order of the learned Chief Judge and directed the application for condonation of delay may be entertained and disposed of on its merit. 16. The facts of the present case are exactly akin to the facts of the aforesaid decision rendered by a Division Bench of the erstwhile High Court of Judicature at Hyderabad. In the case on hand also, the OPSR to set aside the award was filed under Section 34(3) of the Act after 18 days beyond the three months' period stipulated under Section 34(3) of the Act, but within the extended period of 30 days as stipulated in the proviso in the said Section. However, on its being returned, an application for condonation of delay of 18 days was filed. An application to condone delay in re-filing was also filed. The learned District Judge already condoned the delay in re-filing. As in the MSRM, J& JUD J C.R.P.no.849 of 2019 11 instant case, on the OPSR being returned with the objections of the office of the Court below, an application to condone delay was filed thereafter along with an application to condone delay in representation and as the OPSR under Section 34 of the Act was filed within the extended period of 30 days, the mere fact that the application to condone delay was filed thereafter is not fatal and the filing an application to condone the delay in said circumstances would not mean that the OPSR was filed beyond 120 days. Hence, both the questions formulated supra are answered in favour of the petitioner and against the 1st respondent. Accordingly, we find that the first set of contentions of the 1st respondent in the facts & circumstances of the case do not merit consideration and therefore, are liable for rejection. Accordingly, the said contentions stand rejected. 17. The next set of contentions of the 1st respondent on the aspect that the learned District Judge is not having territorial jurisdiction are as under: 'The learned District Judge at Eluru has no jurisdiction to entertain the OPSR. The jurisdiction lies with the Courts at New Delhi, in terms of Clause 27(d) of the Contract Agreement, dated 18.03.2015. The said clause reads as under: 'ARBITRATION: (a) xx xx xxx xxx (b) xx xx xxx xxx (c) xx xx xxx xxx (d) The Contract Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of India and the Courts at New Delhi shall have the exclusive jurisdiction over all disputes arising under, pursuant to and/or in connection with the contract agreement?' The Supreme Court of India was pleased to appoint the sole Arbitrator, by an order, dated 06.10.2017, passed in Special Leave Petition (Civil) no.4450 of 2017. The said order was passed against the judgment & order, dated MSRM, J& JUD J C.R.P.no.849 of 2019 12 20.10.2016, of the Delhi High Court in Arbitration Petition no.326 of 2016 filed under Section 11(6) of the Act. Section 42 of the Act also envisages that all arbitration proceedings and all subsequent applications arising out of that agreement shall be made in that Court, where any application under Part I (which includes Section 11 ) had been made earlier. The 1st respondent earlier approached Delhi High Court by filing application under Section 11(6) of the Act. The Delhi High Court is exclusively having jurisdiction to entertain the present OPSR under Section 34 of the Act. Hence, the OPSR is liable for rejection.' 18. In support of the said contention, reliance is placed on the decision in Emkay Global Financial Services Ltd. V. Giridhar Sondhi4 . In the cited decision, the facts and the ratio are as follows: The respondent therein initiated arbitration proceeding claiming certain amount. The said claim was rejected by the sole Arbitrator by an arbitration award. The respondent filed an application under Section 34 of the Act before the District Court, Delhi. The learned Additional District Judge, having referred to the exclusive jurisdiction clause in the agreement opined that he would have no jurisdiction to proceed further in the matter and therefore, rejected the application under Section 34 of the Act filed in Delhi. In appeal, a learned Single Judge held that the issue of territorial jurisdiction would be decided by the Courts below after the parties let in evidence. Therefore, an appeal was filed before the Supreme Court by the appellant. While allowing the appeal, the Supreme Court held as follows: 'Once Courts in Mumbai have exclusive jurisdiction thanks to the agreement dated 03.07.2008, read with the National Stock Exchange bye-laws, it is clear that Mumbai Courts and the Mumbai Courts alone, before which a Section 34 application can be filed. The arbitration that was conducted at Delhi was 4 AIR 2018 SC 3894 MSRM, J& JUD J C.R.P.no.849 of 2019 13 only at a convenient venue earmarked by the National Stock Exchange, which is evident on a reading of bye-law 4(a)(iv) read with (xiv) contained in Chapter XI.' 19. Per contra, on this aspect of jurisdiction, learned senior counsel for petitioner submitted as follows: - 'The District Court at Eluru is alone having jurisdiction to entertain the OPSR under Section 34 of the Act. He further alternatively contended that when an application for condonation of delay is filed and is pending, the learned District Judge is obliged to deal with the said application and is required to examine as to whether sufficient cause is shown for condonation of delay or not and that the jurisdictional issue with regard to the maintainability of the OPSR has to be considered after the OP is numbered and is entertained by the District Court and not at the stage of considering the application for condonation of delay of 18 days in filing the OPSR.' 20. The first contention that the jurisdictional issue has to be considered after disposal of the application for condonation of delay and after the OP under Section 34 of the Act is entertained cannot be countenanced as in our view unless the Court has the jurisdiction, even the application for condonation of delay in filing the annexed Arbn. OP cannot be condoned. Hence, we find that it is necessary to deal with the issue of jurisdiction. The said issue is a mixed question of fact and law. 21. Firstly, it is necessary to advert to the factual matrix. On 03.02.2015, the petitioner issued request for participation inviting bids for engaging an agency to collect user fees at Tanuku at Km 139.483 for Bommuru - Gundugolanu section from Km 187.6 to Km 81.6 of NH-5 in the State of Andhra Pradesh. The petitioner's bid being highest was accepted on 09.03.2015. Letter of Award was issued. Formal agreement - contract agreement was executed, on 18.03.2015. Petitioner transferred to the claimant the right to collect a user fee for Tanuku plaza for a period of one year in consideration of certain sum payable in weekly instalments at NHAI's Rajahmundry PIU. The MSRM, J& JUD J C.R.P.no.849 of 2019 14 petitioner was thus given right to collect user fee at prescribed rates from the designated vehicles during the term of contract. However, the right was subject to a number of terms and conditions. Be that as it may. The claims related to amount appropriated by the 1st respondent out of performance security deposit, interest, administrative charges, contractor's profit aggregating to certain percentage of total toll collection, interest and legal costs etcetera. Though an arbitrator resolved the dispute sitting at Delhi and passed the award, the 1st respondent could not show that any part of the cause of action arose at a place within the territorial jurisdiction of Delhi High Court. On the other hand, it is manifest from the above narrated factual matrix that the cause of action arose at Tanuku within West Godavari District, that is, within the territorial jurisdiction of the principal District Court, West Godavari District at Eluru. 22. In this context, it is apt to refer to the decision in Maharashtra Chess Association Vs. Union of India (UOI) and Ors. [(2019) 10 SCALE 67] wherein the Supreme Court while observing the issue as to whether a private agreement can oust the writ jurisdiction of a High Court merits further enquiry, held as follows: 'It is a well settled principle of contract law that parties cannot by contract exclude the jurisdiction of all courts. Such a contract would constitute an agreement in restraint of legal proceedings and contravenes Section 28 of the Indian Contract Act 1872. However, where parties to a contract confer jurisdiction on one amongst multiple Courts having proper jurisdiction, to the exclusion of all other Courts, the parties cannot be said to have ousted the jurisdiction of all Courts. Such a contract is valid and will bind the parties to a civil action. This principle was set out in A.B.C. Laminart (P) Limited v. A.P. Agencies, Salem [(1989) 2 SCC 163], ("A B C Laminart") wherein this Court noted: So long as the parties to a contract do not oust the jurisdiction of all the Courts, which would otherwise have jurisdiction to decide MSRM, J& JUD J C.R.P.no.849 of 2019 15 the cause of action under the law it cannot be said that the parties have by their contract ousted the jurisdiction of the Court. If under the law several Courts would have jurisdiction and the parties have agreed to submit to one of these jurisdictions and not to other or others of them, it cannot be said that there is total ouster of jurisdiction. In other words, where the parties to a contract agreed to submit the disputes arising from it to a particular jurisdiction, which would otherwise also be a proper jurisdiction under the law their agreement to the extent they agreed not to submit to other jurisdictions cannot be said to be void as against public policy. If on the other hand the jurisdiction they agreed to submit to would not otherwise be proper jurisdiction to decide disputes arising out of the contract it must be declared void being against public policy. (Emphasis supplied) The decision in A B C Laminart (supra) has been followed in subsequent decisions. Parties cannot by agreement confer jurisdiction on a court which lacks the jurisdiction to adjudicate. But where several courts would have jurisdiction to try the subject matter of the dispute, they can stipulate that a suit be brought exclusively before one of the several courts, to the exclusion of the others.' 23. In the case on hand, the relevant agreement reads as under: "The Contract Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of India and the Courts at New Delhi shall have the exclusive jurisdiction over all disputes arising under, pursuant to and/or in connection with the contract agreement?" 24. Therefore, unless a Court at Delhi otherwise had jurisdiction to decide the arbitration OP and adjudicate on the dispute, the above contract between the parties conferring jurisdiction on the Court at Delhi would be void as per the settled legal position referred to supra, which mandates that parties cannot by agreement confer jurisdiction on a Court, which lacks the jurisdiction to adjudicate. However, under law, that is, as per Sections 16 & MSRM, J& JUD J C.R.P.no.849 of 2019 16 20 of the Code of Civil Procedure , 1908, a proceeding for recovery of money like the one in the case on hand can be instituted where the defendant voluntarily resides or carries on business or personally works for gain. In the case on hand, the answering respondent in the Arbitration case, that is, the petitioner in the Arbn. OP, against which the award was passed by the arbitrator is National Highway Authority of India rep., by its Project Director, New Delhi. In that view of the matter, the Courts at Delhi are having jurisdiction to adjudicate. Hence, the contract between the parties conferring jurisdiction on the Courts at New Delhi cannot be said to be void, in our considered view. 25. For the afore-stated reasons, though the arbitration OP accompanied by an application for condonation of delay in filing the OP is entertainable, yet, in view of the arbitration agreement between the parties conferring exclusive jurisdiction on the Courts at New Delhi, the said OP accompanied by a petition for condonation of delay ought to have been filed in an appropriate Court at Delhi but not in the Principal District Court at Eluru of West Godavari District. In view of our findings, we hold that the impugned order of the learned District Judge dismissing the application for condonation of delay in filing the Arbitration OP is unsustainable as the Court of the learned Principal District Judge at Eluru is not having jurisdiction in view of the arbitration agreement between the parties. As a sequel, we hold that the impugned order is liable to be set aside and the application for condonation of delay is liable to be restored to file and the consequential order, if any, passed rejecting the unnumbered OPSR is also liable to be set aside. Nonetheless, in view of our finding that the arbitration OP accompanied by the application for condonation of delay ought to have been filed in a Court at Delhi, the proper course is now to remit the matter to the learned District Judge for returning the Arbitration OP accompanied by petition for condonation of delay to the petitioner herein MSRM, J& JUD J C.R.P.no.849 of 2019 17 for presentation to proper Court by following the procedure under Section 10A of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. 26. In the result, the Civil Revision Petition is partly allowed and the impugned order is set aside. As a sequel, the application for condonation of delay is restored and the consequential order, if any, passed rejecting the unnumbered OPSR is set aside and the matter is remitted to the learned District Judge for returning the un-numbered Arbitration OP accompanied by petition for condonation of delay to the petitioner herein for presentation to proper Court by following the procedure under Section 10A of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. There shall be no order as to costs. Pending miscellaneous petitions, if any, shall stand closed. _____________________ M. SEETHARAMA MURTI, J ____________ J. UMA DEVI, J 19th September, 2019 RAR THE HON'BLE SRI JUSTICE M.SEETHARAMA MURTI AND THE HON'BLE MS. JUSTICE J.UMA DEVI MSRM, J& JUD J C.R.P.no.849 of 2019 18 Civil Revision Petition no.849 of 2019 (Per Hon'ble Sri Justice M.Seetharama Murti) RAR DATED: 19-09-2019
